title: The structure of instability in moduli theory
speaker: Daniel Halpern-Leistner
abstract: In many examples of moduli stacks which come equipped with a notion of stable points, one tests stability by considering "iso-trivial one parameter degenerations" of a point in the stack. To such a degeneration one can often associate a real number which measures "how destabilizing" it is, and in these situations one can ask the question of whether there is a "maximal destabilizing" or "canonically destabilizing" degeneration of a given unstable point.

proof_stages.label: Moduli-theoretic instability problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up moduli functors/stacks, explains why naive representability and finite-type boundedness fail, and formulates the goal of extracting a semistable open subproblem together with a structured stratification of the unstable locus.

proof_stages.label: Localization motivation and derived strata
proof_stages.summary: A virtual-localization problem for quotient stacks is used to motivate why instability strata need a modular interpretation, since smooth Bialynicki-Birula strata give finite correction terms but singular strata require a derived thickening to restore the needed negativity of weights.

proof_stages.label: Known canonical destabilizations as maps from A^1/G_m
proof_stages.summary: Hilbert-Mumford-Kempf theory in GIT and the Harder-Narasimhan filtration of vector bundles are reinterpreted as the existence of a canonical map from A^1/G_m to the moduli stack, based at the unstable object and maximizing a numerical invariant.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological numerical invariant
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes that a general instability function is obtained from classes in H^2 and H^4 of the stack by assigning to a degeneration f the real number f^*l/sqrt(f^*b), with semistability and maximal destabilization phrased in terms of this invariant.

proof_stages.label: Degeneration space of a point
proof_stages.summary: To make the maximization problem tractable, maps from A^1/G_m and higher toric test objects into the stack are organized, up to ramified covers and toric restriction maps, into a combinatorial/topological degeneration space on which the numerical invariant extends continuously.

proof_stages.label: Convexity and weak reductivity
proof_stages.summary: For affine quotient stacks and more generally for weakly reductive stacks, degeneration spaces are convex in the sense that rational points are joined by unique rational one-simplices, so convexity of the numerical function reduces uniqueness of the maximizer to a boundedness problem.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic criterion via mapping stacks
proof_stages.summary: Using algebraicity of the mapping stack Map(A^1/G_m,X), weak reductivity is recast as a valuative-properness property of the evaluation-at-one map, explaining why affine quotient stacks and sheaf-moduli stacks satisfy the desired uniqueness behavior.

examples.note: Taking higher and higher powers of a fixed ample line bundle on a smooth curve illustrates why the full vector-bundle moduli functor is not finite type and motivates passing to semistable loci and strata.

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the two scalar G_m choices, identifying the origin stratum versus the case where the whole affine space is unstable and the semistable locus is empty
